phrasalVerbs.definition: To become cooler or make something cooler.
examples: Let the soup cool down before you eat it.
We went swimming to cool down after the long walk.
The weather is finally starting to cool down.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If something dries up or is dried up, all the water comes out of it.
examples: The river dried up during the summer drought.
Leave the dishes to dry up on the rack.
The puddles dried up quickly in the hot sun.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make something hot; to become hot.
examples: Can you heat up the leftovers for dinner?
The room started to heat up when we turned on the heater.
Heat up the oil before adding the vegetables.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make food warm again; to prepare for a sport or activity by doing gentle exercises.
examples: I'll warm up some soup for lunch.
Athletes always warm up before a competition.
Can you warm up the milk for the baby?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To add water to a drink or liquid in order to make it less strong; to dilute.
examples: This juice is too sweet—I need to water it down.
The bartender watered down the drinks.
Don't water down the sauce too much.

fillInExercises.sentence: Be careful! The coffee is very hot. Let it ___ ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cool down' means to become less hot.

fillInExercises.sentence: The plants ___ up because we forgot to water them.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Dry up' means to lose all moisture.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you ___ ___ the pizza in the microwave?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Heat up' means to make something hot.

fillInExercises.sentence: I'll ___ up some pasta from yesterday for dinner.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Warm up' means to make food warm again.

fillInExercises.sentence: This sauce is too thick. I need to ___ it ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Water down' means to dilute by adding water.

fillInExercises.sentence: It's too hot outside. I need to ___ down with a cold drink.
The athletes need to ___ ___ before the match.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Warm up' also means to prepare for exercise.

fillInExercises.sentence: ___ up the oven to 200 degrees before baking.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Heat up' means to make something hot.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Can you [heat up|heat up] the soup while I make the salad?

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Sure! Should I also [warm up|warm up] the bread?

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Yes, please. But let the soup [cool down|cool down] a bit before serving.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Okay. This juice seems strong—should I [water down|water down] it?

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Yes, add some water. Leave the herbs on the counter to [dry up|dry up].

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Got it! Dinner will be ready soon.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: It's so hot today! I need to [cool down|cool down] with something cold.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Me too. Let me [warm up|warm up] some iced tea for us.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: You mean [heat up|heat up]? We want it cold, not hot!

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Ha! You're right. I'll just [water down|water down] the lemonade instead.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Perfect. The garden is really [drying up|dry up] in this heat.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: True. Let's wait until the evening when it [cools down|cool down] to water the plants.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Good idea. The weather should [cool down|cool down] after sunset.
